content.text: Key insightThe length of a vector is how many elements are “in use”.The capacity of a vector is how many elements have been allocated in memory.
The length of a vector is how many elements are “in use”.The capacity of a vector is how many elements have been allocated in memory.

list: Thestd::vectoracquires new memory with capacity for the desired number of elements. These elements are value-initialized.The elements in the old memory are copied (or moved, if possible) into the new memory. The old memory is then returned to the system.The capacity and length of thestd::vectorare set to the new values.

The elements in the old memory are copied (or moved, if possible) into the new memory. The old memory is then returned to the system.The capacity and length of thestd::vectorare set to the new values.
The capacity and length of thestd::vectorare set to the new values.

content.text: Related contentThe process of acquiring new memory at runtime is called dynamic memory allocation. We cover this in lesson19.1 -- Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete.
The process of acquiring new memory at runtime is called dynamic memory allocation. We cover this in lesson19.1 -- Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete.
Key insightReallocation is typically expensive. Avoid unnecessary reallocations.
Reallocation is typically expensive. Avoid unnecessary reallocations.

content.code:
Capacity: 5  Length: 5
0 1 2 3 4 
Capacity: 5  Length: 3
0 1 2 
Capacity: 5  Length: 5
0 1 2 0 0

content.text: Key insightTracking capacity separately from length allows thestd::vectorto avoid some reallocations when length is changed.
Tracking capacity separately from length allows thestd::vectorto avoid some reallocations when length is changed.
WarningA subscript is only valid if it is between 0 and the vector’s length (not its capacity)!
A subscript is only valid if it is between 0 and the vector’s length (not its capacity)!

On the author’s machine, this produces the following result:Capacity: 1000  Length: 1000
Capacity: 1000  Length: 0
Capacity: 0  Length: 0As you can see, whenv.shrink_to_fit()was called, the vector reallocated its capacity to 0, freeing up the memory for 1000 elements.Quiz timeQuestion #1What do the length and capacity of a std::vector represent?Show SolutionLength is the number of elements currently in use.Capacity is the number of elements that have storage allocated.Why are length and capacity separate values?Show SolutionCapacity is tracked separately so that the vector can avoid some reallocations when the length is changed.Are the valid indices forstd::vectorbased on length or capacity?Show SolutionLength.Next lesson16.11std::vector and stack behaviorBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson16.9Array indexing and length using enumerators

Capacity: 1000  Length: 0
Capacity: 0  Length: 0
